aspects.name: Evaluate function values
aspects.description: Substitute a given input into a nonlinear rule and compute the output, or solve for the input that produces a given output. The student executes the function as a procedure; no interpretation of context or graph features is required.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 differs from A2 in that A1 is context-free numerical evaluation; if the stem asks for the meaning of a value, it is A2. A1 differs from A3 because A1 takes the equation as given and computes; A3 reasons about graph features (intercepts, vertex, behavior).

types.name: Direct substitution into f(x)
types.description: A nonlinear function f (polynomial, radical, rational, absolute-value, or exponential) is defined; the student must compute f(c) for a numeric c.
types.core_skill_demand: Evaluate a closed-form nonlinear expression at a specific numeric input by substitution and arithmetic simplification.

characteristics: single-term or two-term rule like x^n+k or a√x+k
small whole-number input
no fraction or negative-exponent base
rule uses a fractional / negative-exponent or absolute-value form
input is a non-integer or larger value
may include exponential f(0) requiring base-rule recall
input is a parameter expression like 15a or 7a forcing absolute-value sign analysis
computation requires combining two function evaluations (sum, product, composition)
hard.example_stem: g(x) = |x|/a − 14 with a<0. What is g(15a) · g(7a)?

must_preserve: explicit closed-form rule for f
single numeric (or symbolic) input to evaluate
no contextual interpretation in the stem
must_vary: surface function family (polynomial, radical, rational, absolute value, exponential)
common_bugs: cloning a quadratic but choosing an input that yields a non-integer (drifts difficulty)
swapping √x for x^(1/2) without preserving domain
replacing a^x with a*x — loses exponential character (aspect drift to linear)

types.name: Solve f(x) = k for x
types.description: Given a nonlinear function and a target output value, find the input x. May involve square, cube, radical, rational, or absolute-value functions.
types.core_skill_demand: Invert a nonlinear function for a specific output by isolating x — squaring, taking roots, clearing denominators, or testing candidate values.

characteristics: single-step inversion: x^2=k, √x=k, or n^c with small numbers
easy.example_stem: f(x) = 8√x. For what x is f(x) = 48?

characteristics: cube root or two-step inversion (e.g., 7n^3 = 56)
medium.example_stem: p(n) = 7n^3. What is n when p(n)=56?

characteristics: absolute-value equation with parameter k on both sides, requiring case analysis
hard.example_stem: f(x)=|59 − 2x|. For which k does f(k)=3k?

must_preserve: target output stated as a single number or expression
exactly one stated function rule
must_vary: choice of inverse operation (root, division, case split)
common_bugs: picking a target that yields multiple solutions when only one is expected (e.g., x^2 = 25 with no domain restriction)
removing the absolute value in the hard variant — collapses to linear

aspects.name: Interpret value or feature in context
aspects.description: Given a contextual nonlinear model (exponential growth/decay, projectile quadratic, etc.), identify the real-world meaning of a specific value, coefficient, point, vertex, or intercept. The student translates symbols to meaning rather than computing.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 always asks 'what does X represent / best interpretation'. If the stem asks for a numeric answer (no interpretation), it is A1, A3, or A4 depending on demand.

types.name: Interpret f(c)=v statement
types.description: Stem gives a contextual exponential or quadratic and asks for the best interpretation of a specific evaluation (e.g., 'P(8) ≈ 32.91').
types.core_skill_demand: Map the input variable to its real-world meaning (time, count), map the output to its real-world meaning (population, revenue, height), and pick the option whose units and quantities match both.

characteristics: evaluation at t=0 (initial value) or simple year shift
context clearly stated and units explicit
non-zero input requiring offset reasoning (e.g., 'years after 1957')
half-life or decay parameter requires understanding rate
interpretation of a coordinate (x,y) on a quadratic model, requiring both axes to be translated
interpretation tied to vertex of a contextual quadratic

must_preserve: a fully described real-world context
an explicit evaluation statement or specific point
the 'best interpretation' question form
common_bugs: dropping the context to produce a context-free clone (categorical drift to A1)
writing distractors that swap inputs and outputs identically — leaves only one plausible answer

types.name: Interpret coefficient or constant
types.description: Stem asks for the real-world meaning of a specific number embedded in the model (initial value, growth factor, vertex coordinate, y-intercept).
types.core_skill_demand: Identify which structural role a constant plays in the symbolic form (initial value, multiplier per unit time, maximum, axis crossing) and state the corresponding real-world quantity.

characteristics: constant is the obvious initial value or y-intercept of an exponential
context fully spelled out with units
easy.example_stem: y-intercept of a depreciation graph — interpret in context.

characteristics: interpretation of a base or initial-value constant in an explicit exponential
interpretation of vertex of a contextual quadratic
interpret y-intercept of a decay model in context (must reason about t=0)
interpret vertex of a quadratic model where vertex coordinates are not the maximum height alone but a paired (time, height)
interpret positive x-intercept of a projectile model
hard.example_stem: f(t)=8000(0.65)^t coupon model — best interpretation of y-intercept?

must_preserve: the targeted constant is structurally identifiable (initial value, growth factor, vertex coord, intercept)
real-world variable mappings
common_bugs: changing the constant so it no longer plays the named structural role
writing four interpretations that are all numerically true but only one matches role — verify against template's must_preserve

aspects.name: Analyze graph features
aspects.description: Identify or compute key features of a nonlinear graph — intercepts, vertex, max/min, intervals of increase, asymptotes — either from a provided graph or from an equation. The student reads structure off of geometry/equation form.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 differs from A2 because A3 returns numeric features (coordinates, values), not contextual interpretations. A3 differs from A1 because A3 reasons about graph features, not about computing f at a given point.

types.name: Read intercept or point from graph
types.description: A graph is shown; the student identifies the y-intercept, x-intercept, or a labeled point.
types.core_skill_demand: Visually decode a graph (exponential, polynomial, parabola, rational) to read a specific intercept or point coordinate.

characteristics: intercept is a labeled grid point
single-curve graph
easy.example_stem: What is the y-intercept of the graph shown?

characteristics: graph shows multiple features (vertex + intercepts) and stem requires choosing the right one
may involve a translation
medium.example_stem: Graph translated up 4 units — which is the resulting graph?

characteristics: graph paired with stem requiring choice among options that include near-distractor coordinates

must_preserve: a graph (svg/figure) with at least one clearly identifiable target point
stem requesting a specific intercept / coordinate value
common_bugs: replacing svg with text-only description (drops aspect)
moving the y-intercept off integer values without indicating approximation

types.name: y-intercept / point from equation
types.description: An equation is given (often quadratic, exponential, or polynomial); the student computes the y-intercept (or another point) without a graph.
types.core_skill_demand: Evaluate the equation at x=0 (or another given x) and recognize the result as the intercept.

characteristics: evaluate y = x^2 + c at x=0; or f(0) for a polynomial
easy.example_stem: y = x^2 + 31 — what is the y-coordinate of the y-intercept?

characteristics: exponential with non-obvious base; need to recall a^0=1
context-free polynomial of higher degree
transformed exponential (exponent with constant offset), or shifted exponential where (−8)(2)^x + 22 type form
asking for equivalent form that displays the intercept as a coefficient/constant
hard.example_stem: y = 576^(2x+2) — which equivalent form shows the y-intercept as a constant?

must_preserve: explicit equation
intercept (or specific x) question
common_bugs: asking for a y-intercept where x=0 is outside the stated domain
replacing exponential with quadratic but leaving the 'as a base' option distractors

types.name: Vertex or extreme value of quadratic
types.description: Stem asks for vertex coordinates, the minimum or maximum value of a quadratic, or the x-value at which a quadratic reaches its extremum.
types.core_skill_demand: Either complete the square, use −b/(2a), or use factored-form symmetry to locate the vertex of a quadratic.

characteristics: vertex visible on graph
rule already in vertex form
easy.example_stem: Graph of y=f(x) parabola through (−1,5),(0,2),(1,5). Vertex?

characteristics: rule in form y = x^2 + c (vertex on y-axis)
rule p(x)+k = x^2 with single rearrangement
medium.example_stem: g(x)=x^2+55. What is the minimum value of g?

characteristics: rule given in factored form (x−p)(x+q); requires axis-of-symmetry midpoint
rule in standard form with two non-trivial coefficients requiring −b/(2a)
stem asks for x-value of vertex of a composition g(x)=f(x+5)
interpretive variants ('which equivalent form displays the maximum?')
hard.example_stem: f(x)=(x−14)(x+19). For what x does f reach minimum?

must_preserve: quadratic form (factored, vertex, or standard)
vertex-related question
must_vary: which form (factored vs standard vs vertex)
common_bugs: converting factored form to standard form before cloning (simplifies the hard variant)
choosing roots that give a non-integer axis of symmetry then asking for integer answer
swapping minimum/maximum without flipping leading coefficient sign

types.name: x-intercept / zeros of polynomial
types.description: Stem asks for an x-intercept, root, factor, or zero of a polynomial (quadratic, cubic, or higher-degree). May be from factored form, standard form, or table.
types.core_skill_demand: Use factored form directly, factor the quadratic, or use the factor theorem (a root corresponds to factor (x−r)) to identify zeros.

characteristics: (not observed)
factored quadratic f(x)=(x+a)(x−b); read off roots
factor-theorem question naming integer roots
stem asks 'which is NOT an x-intercept'
polynomial of degree ≥3 in factored form with repeated factor — requires distinguishing distinct vs total roots
table-based factor identification using polynomial values
two-parameter equation in factored form with constraint about x-intercepts
contextual quadratic where one of two roots must be selected as the physical answer

must_preserve: polynomial (≥quadratic) presented in identifiable form
root/intercept question
common_bugs: expanding a factored cubic into standard form (loses hard variant character)
writing a cubic where two of the three roots collapse without using that as the difficulty lever intentionally

types.name: Other graph-feature reasoning
types.description: Asks about interval of increase/decrease, asymptote, symmetry, or which equation matches a described graph shape.
types.core_skill_demand: Connect described graph behavior (multiple turning points, end behavior, asymptotes) to algebraic structure (degree, leading coefficient sign, factor multiplicity).

characteristics: choose graph from a table of values
identify horizontal asymptote from labeled graph
easy.example_stem: Table of (x,y) — which graph could represent the relationship?

characteristics: identify interval where graph is increasing on a labeled parabola
match equation to a multi-turn polynomial graph
translate rational graph
medium.example_stem: Parabola y=f(x) — for which interval is y increasing?

characteristics: polynomial graph with multiple turning points and crossing/touching distinction
rational function with asymptotes — identify equation form
exponential with horizontal asymptote — count intersections with horizontal line
hard.example_stem: Graph y=f(x)=ab^x+c crosses y=−7 how many times?

must_preserve: graph or precise verbal description of graph
feature-identification question (not numeric evaluation)
common_bugs: dropping the asymptote when copying a rational/exponential — destroys answer logic
converting 'increasing interval' to 'maximum' (drift to T3)

aspects.name: Model nonlinear situation
aspects.description: Construct (not interpret) a nonlinear function or equation that represents a described real-world situation — usually exponential growth/decay, geometric area/volume, or quadratic projectile motion. Output is an equation or value derived from one.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 produces an equation/model from a description; A2 takes an equation/model and interprets one of its values. A4 differs from A6 because A4's parameters are fully specified in the description; A6 has unknown parameters to be solved from additional constraints.

types.name: Build exponential growth/decay model
types.description: Given initial value and a per-period multiplicative change, write the function or compute a value from it.
types.core_skill_demand: Translate 'increased by p% per period' or 'doubles every k years' into A·r^x or A·2^(x/k), preserving the per-period structure.

characteristics: doubling/halving language with explicit period
stem identifies initial value and number of periods clearly
easy.example_stem: Account opened at $890 and doubles every 10 years. Equation for value M(t)?

characteristics: percent-change phrasing (e.g., '7% greater than previous month')
value-after-n-periods question requiring two-step compute
table of (t, amount) implying compound rate
medium.example_stem: Newsletter grows 7% per month from 1300; equation for c(m)?

characteristics: non-annual period rewrite required ('m months after census' from a yearly model, or 'p% every 18 months' from yearly)
150%-more vs 150%-OF distractor
exponent form (1.84)^(x/4) needing identification of effective per-unit rate
double-time question from f(t)=A·2^(t/k) requiring k itself
hard.example_stem: P(t)=90000(1.06)^t years — best model for m months?

must_preserve: explicit initial value
explicit per-period multiplicative rate
discrete period structure
common_bugs: confusing '150% more' (×2.5) with '150% of' (×1.5)
writing P(t)=A·r·t instead of A·r^t (linearization drift, categorical error)
for the hard rewrite type, simplifying the unit conversion (e.g., forgetting (r^(1/12))^m)

types.name: Quadratic projectile / motion model
types.description: Object launched/dropped/thrown; build or use a quadratic h(t) to find initial height, time to ground, time to a specific height, or max-height time.
types.core_skill_demand: Read coefficients of a quadratic in t (or build one from initial height, max height, time of max), recognize that the constant term is initial height and that −b/(2a) gives the time of vertex.

characteristics: (not observed)
read constant term of h(t)=at^2+bt+c as initial height
use given quadratic to find time-to-ground via factoring
medium.example_stem: h = −4.9t^2 + 7t + 9 — height from which object was kicked?

characteristics: build the quadratic from verbal description of vertex and intercepts (e.g., 'launched from 7m, reached 51.1m at t=3')
use vertex-form symmetry to find time for return to a given height
hard.example_stem: Projectile launched from 7m, max 51.1m at t=3 — when back to 7m?

must_preserve: physical motion context (projectile, dropped object, dive)
quadratic structure (downward parabola for gravity-bound motion)
must_vary: which feature is asked (initial, max, time-to-ground)
common_bugs: mismatching signs of the leading coefficient with described concavity
using vertex form's k as 'time of max' instead of 'max height'

types.name: Geometric area/volume model
types.description: Build or use a quadratic/cubic expression for area, volume, or perimeter when one side is expressed in terms of another (length = 2×width, length = width+k).
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize multiplicative relationships among geometric dimensions, encode them in a single variable, and solve a quadratic for the dimension.

characteristics: (not observed)
one dimension in terms of the other; given area, solve for width
build expression w(w+9) given factored form description
medium.example_stem: Area = w(w+9); which expression is the length?

characteristics: product of two unknowns with two simultaneous constraints
3D volume model (V = h·l·w where l = w+1)
uses an area-as-function form V(x), then matches against multiple polynomial options
kinetic-energy / KE-style nonlinear application
hard.example_stem: Rectangular prism, height 9, length x = width+7. V(x)?

must_preserve: geometric (or geometric-analog) context
two related dimensions giving a quadratic/cubic
must_vary: which dimension or quantity is requested
whether asked for an expression or numeric solution
common_bugs: picking dimensions that produce non-integer answers in 'find width' variant
writing V = lwh but forgetting to substitute the dimension relation, leaving multiple variables

aspects.name: Transformations & equivalent forms
aspects.description: Apply or recognize transformations (vertical/horizontal shifts, replacing x by x+k), or rewrite a nonlinear expression into an equivalent form that exposes a feature. Output is a new function or feature value derived via algebraic restructuring.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 alters the algebraic form (shifts, equivalent rewrites); A3 reads features from a fixed form; A4 builds a model from context.

types.name: Vertical/horizontal shift
types.description: Given y=f(x), find or describe y = f(x)+k or y = f(x+h), or evaluate a translated function at a point.
types.core_skill_demand: Apply the rule 'f(x+h) shifts left by h; f(x)+k shifts up by k' and compute the resulting equation or value.

characteristics: graph translation in one direction with multiple-choice graphs
easy.example_stem: Parabola translated up 4 — which graph results?

characteristics: function rule shifted down by k; produce new equation
translate a table-based polynomial output
medium.example_stem: f(x)=7x^3; g shifts f down 2. g(x)=?

characteristics: composition with shift in input (f(x+k)) on factored polynomial — must redistribute factors
vertex of translated quadratic g(x)=f(x+5)
evaluate g(0) after up-shift of cubic
given factored cubic and shift, find sum of new x-intercepts

must_preserve: a source function f and a transformation (shift) description
a target output (equation, value, or feature)
common_bugs: confusing f(x+k) with f(x)+k (horizontal vs vertical)
shifting roots of a factored cubic in the wrong direction (sign flip)

types.name: Equivalent form revealing feature
types.description: Stem asks which equivalent form of a nonlinear function displays a target feature (vertex, max/min, y-intercept, growth rate) as a constant, coefficient, or base.
types.core_skill_demand: Choose between standard, vertex, factored, and base-rebased forms by recognizing which form makes the target feature explicit.

characteristics: (not observed)
choose among forms of an exponential to expose f(0)=c
medium.example_stem: For exponential f, f(0)=c. Which form shows c as coefficient?

characteristics: max/min of a transformed exponential must be expressed in a chosen equivalent form
rewrite of (1.84)^(x/4) as (1+p/100)^x and identify p
rewrite as base-2 with offset exponent to show y-intercept
hard.example_stem: f(x)=−6x^2+60x−126. Which equivalent form displays the max?

must_preserve: targeted feature (max, min, intercept, growth rate)
multiple equivalent forms among answer choices
common_bugs: providing equivalent-form options that are not actually equivalent (verification step needed)
having two options that both display the feature — ambiguous correct answer

aspects.name: Reason about unknown parameters
aspects.description: Given a nonlinear function with one or more unknown constants and additional structural conditions (passes through point, has stated vertex, has given intercept), determine the value of a parameter or a combination of parameters.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A6 always requires solving for unknown parameters using stated structural conditions. If parameters are given numerically and the task is to evaluate or interpret, it is A1/A2/A3 instead.

types.name: Solve for parameter from condition
types.description: Given f(x) with constants a, b (or c) and one or more conditions like 'passes through (p,q)' or 'has vertex at (h,k)' or 'intercept at (0,n)', find the parameter or a combination.
types.core_skill_demand: Substitute the stated conditions into the symbolic form to produce equations in the unknown parameters, then solve (typically a system of 1–2 equations).

characteristics: (not observed)
one unknown, one substitution condition (e.g., f(3)=value gives a)
linear relation a^n = k solvable by inspection
medium.example_stem: y = 3600·a^x; what was the initial number?

characteristics: two unknowns, two simultaneous conditions (e.g., y-intercept and a passing point)
vertex constraint plus extra structural info (intercept sign, value sign of k)
parameter recovered from indirect feature (intercepts symmetric around vertex)
exponential with subtraction f(x)=a^x+b and two passing points

must_preserve: function form with at least one unknown parameter
at least one structural condition (point, intercept, vertex) that pins down the parameter
must_vary: whether condition is point-based or feature-based
common_bugs: providing conditions that under- or over-determine the parameters
designing a quadratic problem where the requested parameter combination (e.g., a+b+c) happens to equal one of the conditions trivially
for exponential a^x+b, choosing constraints that admit a≤0 or a=1 (degenerate)

skill_wide_notes: 240 originals span 60 Easy / 96 Medium / 84 Hard. Frequency counts are approximate primary-type assignments; a handful of originals fit two types and have been counted in only one. Easy items are heavily concentrated in A1.T1 (direct substitution), A2.T1 (interpret f(c)=v), and A3.T1 (read intercept from graph). Hard items concentrate in A3.T3 (vertex from factored/standard form), A5 (transformations & equivalent forms), and A6.T1 (solve for parameters). Quadratic vertex problems (A3.T3) are the most prolific Hard type. Exponential modeling (A4.T1) appears at every difficulty level — the lever shifts from simple doubling (Easy) to percent-change (Medium) to period-rewrite (Hard). Graph-dependent items (A3.T1, A3.T5) are reserved almost entirely for Easy and Medium; Hard items in A3 are usually equation-only.
